clues.clue: This fist-sized muscular organ pumps roughly 100,000 times a day.
Blood returns first to these two upper receiving chambers of the heart.
This largest artery carries oxygen-rich blood out of the left ventricle to the body.
These vessels feed the heart muscle itself, and their blockage triggers heart attacks.
This natural pacemaker in the right atrium sets the heartbeat, abbreviated SA.
Often called the 'silent killer,' this elevated force in the arteries is read in mmHg.
This waxy blood lipid, tracked as LDL and HDL, can clog arteries when high.
Inhaling tobacco is a leading modifiable cause of cardiovascular disease.
This state of excess body fat, gauged partly by BMI, strains the cardiovascular system.
High blood pressure, high sugar, abdominal fat and bad lipids together form this 'metabolic' cluster.
A heart attack, the death of heart muscle from blocked flow, is medically this, abbreviated MI.
This irregular, rapid quivering of the upper chambers sharply raises stroke risk; it's nicknamed A-fib.
Chest pain from reduced blood flow to the heart, often on exertion, is this.
When the heart can't pump efficiently, fluid backs up and breathlessness results in this failing condition.
The buildup of fatty plaque that hardens and narrows arteries is this process.
Abbreviated ECG or EKG, this test records the heart's electrical activity.
A healthy resting pulse runs about 60 to 100 of these per minute.
This ultrasound of the heart shows its chambers and valves in motion.
A blood-pressure reading of 120 over 80 is classified as this.
A treadmill 'stress test' looks for this reduced blood flow to the heart during exertion.
Cholesterol-lowering drugs such as atorvastatin belong to this class ending in '-statin.'
A low daily dose of this common pain reliever thins the blood to help prevent clots.
Drugs like lisinopril, ending in '-pril,' lower pressure by blocking a key enzyme abbreviated ACE.
A small mesh tube can be placed to prop open a narrowed artery; name that tube.
This open-heart surgery, abbreviated CABG, reroutes blood around clogged coronary arteries using grafts.

final.clue: This plant-forward eating pattern named for a sea region is widely recommended for heart health.
